data.title: 10-Year U.S. Treasury Yield 'Fair Value' Estimate: 11 April, 2025
data.description:
The “fair value” for the US 10-year Treasury yield edged higher in March vs. the previous month and is now approaching the 4.0% mark – the highest estimate in 16 years. The analysis is based on the average of three models run by CapitalSpectator.com.

The current average monthly fair-value estimate is 3.91%, which remains below the actual 10-year yield. In yesterday’s trading, the benchmark rate rose to 4.44% (Apr. 10).

As discussed on these pages in recent history, a market premium for the 10-year rate has persisted since late-2022. The latest increase in the average fair-value estimate suggests that the actual 10-year yield will remain relatively steady if not trend higher in the near term.

History reminds that the market prices in an evolving run of premiums and discounts relative to the fair-value estimate, as shown by the spread in the chart below.

The current premium has pulled back from a relatively high level recently, but looks set to continue in positive terrain for the near term. Several factors are expected to maintain the market premium, including renewed inflation risk related to a global trade war.

Consumer inflation eased in March, but the data does not yet reflect the higher prices that are expected to arrive due to increases in US import tariffs.

“I think it’ll take some time for the inflationary shock to work its way into the system,” said Preston Caldwell, chief US economist at Morningstar. “At first, [inflation data] might look better than it will be eventually.”

The softer CPI data for last month “is good news for the Fed, but the data seems stale,” advised US Bank chief economist Beth Ann Bovino.

An opposing macro force that could reduce the market premium by driving the 10-year yield lower: slower growth if not recession, a risk factor that’s also heightened by higher tariffs.

The key question is whether inflation risk or slow growth/recession risk dominate for the 10-year yield’s path ahead. At the moment, it appears that the two risks are more or less balanced as the market churns in a range vs. recent history.

The wide swings in the 10-year yield in recent days suggest that investors are struggling to price in which risk is the bigger threat – inflation vs. slower growth/recession. A breakout above the recent peak (roughly 4.80%), or below the recent trough (3.90%), would signal a consensus in the crowd’s ex ante risk evaluation.

data.title: Dan Ivascyn On Bond Investing In A Turbulent Global Market
data.description:
Transcript

Dan Ivascyn: You mentioned a lot of volatility in equity markets and credit spreads have widened, a bit. Credit spreads have widened a bit. But you have very, very strong technicals, arguably still a little bit of complacency or supply demand imbalances. They're keeping spreads tight. That's an area which we may talk about more, but where we remain more cautious. But bonds, and I think it's important to put this into perspective, because the last few days have been rough.

But the ten-year Treasury yields back to the levels that we saw in mid-February. Just a few quarters ago, we had higher levels. And then the 30-year bonds retraced back to a level generally near where we were in January.

So it's been volatile. But we still have a situation where treasury yields, although they're high, they haven't become extremely dislocated yet. But again, that's an area that we're focusing on that you asked about the path for fixed income, as we've been saying time and time again. Yeah, it's rough. Yes, it can be a bumpy road.

But starting yields in the higher quality areas of the fixed income markets are attractive. Even if inflation were to be sustained comfortably above central bank targets. So this sell off has again created opportunity without having to lock up your money for an extended period of time, without having to take a lot of economically sensitive risk.

And again, it looks attractive absolute and relative to other segments of the market.

data.title: Hochschild Mining: Printing Cash At $3,100 Gold
data.description:
Introduction

As mentioned in my previous article on Hochschild Mining (OTCQX:HCHDF) (OTCPK:HCHDY), this is the perfect time to get a new gold mine up and running. Hochschild has now completed the construction of a new mine in Brazil which will contribute approximately 100,000 ounces of gold to this year’s production numbers. With gold above $3100 and the futures, indicating a price north of $3300/oz next year, the timing works well for Hochschild.

This company has its primary listing on the London Stock Exchange, so I'd strongly recommend to trade in the company's shares on that exchange, given the superior liquidity. The ticker symbol on the LSE is HOC and the average daily volume is approximately 2.8 million shares per day.

Ending the year on a strong note, thanks to the metal prices

During 2024, the company produced approximately 8.5 million ounces of silver as well as just under 250,000 ounces of gold compared to the preceding year. This represents an 11% decrease for the silver production and a 32% increase in the gold production. Given the very strong gold price lately, it’s nice to see the company being able to immediately benefit from that, and that was immediately noticeable in the financial results. Hochschild reported a 37% revenue increase and as the operating expenses increased at a slower pace, the adjusted EBITDA increased by in excess of 50%.

The all-in sustaining costs increased as well, and Hochschild’s published 2024 AISC was $1638 per ounce gold-equivalent which was about $100/oz higher than the previously issued cost guidance. This was related to a slower ramp-up than anticipated at the Mara Rosa mine, while the inflation in Argentina also played a role in the performance of the 51% owned San Jose mine.

The total reported revenue in 2024 was approximately $948M, which allowed Hochschild to report a gross profit of $342M, an increase of almost 85% compared to the preceding year.

As the income statement above shows, Hochschild reported an EBIT of just under $208M. That’s a very strong improvement over the FY 2023 result, as the amount of impairment expenses was much higher in 2023.

Looking at the bottom-line result, the reported net profit was almost $114M of which $97M was attributable to Hochschild’s shareholders with almost $17M attributable to non-controlling interests. This includes a net impact of almost $20M on the Hochschild level, and the income statement above does a good job in highlighting these non-recurring items. Adjusted for those items, Hochschild’s attributable net profit was almost $117M, representing approximately $0.23 per share.

Just like in the previous article, I’m very interested in the company’s cash flow profile. Before jumping to Hochschild’s cash flow statement, I wanted to start with one of the footnotes considering the company’s cash flow statement starts with the $365M in ‘cash generated from operations’. The details on how Hochschild achieved that result can be found below.

As you notice, the $365M in cash from operations includes a $29M investment in the working capital position, and that should be added back to the equation to find the normalized cash flows.

On the other hand, we should deduct the $5M in lease payments, and we should deduct the $55M in additional taxes that were part of the income statement but haven’t been paid out in cash yet.

This results in an adjusted operating cash flow of approximately $290M, and approximately $280M after taking the impact of the non-controlling interests into account.

As shown above, the total capex was approximately $234M (and this, of course, also still includes the remainder of the growth capex spent at Mara Rosa). About $20M of the capex was related to an intangible investment in legal rights (related to the permitting for exploration, development and production). Excluding that intangible investment, Hochschild generated about $76M in free cash flow. That doesn’t sound impressive, but let’s not forget the net realized price was just under $2350/oz for the gold. As market prices have increased, we can expect the average realized price to increase as well.

What can we expect in 2025?

The company also provided its outlook for this year, and Hochschild expects to produce 350,000-378,000 ounces of gold-equivalent thanks to the important contribution from the new Mara Rosa gold mine, which will produce about 100,000 ounces of gold this year. The Inmaculada mine in Peru had an exceptionally strong 2024 with an attributable production of just over 220,000 ounces of gold, but this isn’t sustainable, and the 2025 production guidance assumes a production decrease of 7-8%.

The all-in sustaining cost is estimated at $1587-1687/oz, so at the current gold price, the margin will be around $1300/oz using a $1700/oz AISC. Multiplied by the lower end of the production guidance, results in a net cash flow of $455M at the mine level.

Of course, we still have to deduct the net finance expenses (estimated at $15M) and the tax (approximately $125-140M) resulting in approximately $300M in normalized sustaining free cash flow. Of course, Hochschild will also expense money on exploration and pursue other growth projects, while there will be G&A on the corporate level as well. But taking these elements into account, at $3000 gold, I anticipate the net free cash flow to come in at around $225-250M.

Given the current share count of 514.5M shares outstanding, this would represent approximately $0.42 per share after also taking the cash flow and income attributable to non-controlling interests into account. This represents approximately 33 pence per share, which means the current share price of 277 pence still provides some upside potential.

Investment thesis

Hochschild’s share price has performed very well since my previous article, and I think the ‘easy gains’ have been made. I still see upside potential, so investors looking to retain exposure to the gold and silver prices may retain a long position or a portion of their long position.

It goes without saying the markets are pretty volatile these days, and it probably wouldn’t be wise to chase the stock, but every day the company can sell gold at $3100+ per ounce is a good day. I currently have no position in Hochschild but given the current strength in the gold price I should perhaps go long again.

data.title: Why RENK Group AG Is Definitely On My 'Radar' Here
data.description:
The company goes Dear readers/followers,

I have made no secret about my interest in and the appeal of the German and European defense industry. In fact, many of my investments for the past few years have been centered around these segments, with major investments in companies including Rolls-Royce (OTCPK:RYCEF), Rheinmetall AG (OTCPK:RNMBY), Leonardo (OTCPK:FINMY), and others. Every single investment in European defense I have made has turned out excellent.

I would go so far as to argue that I have, unfortunately, missed out on many of them as well. That is something I intend to change on a forward basis.

In my work, I came across a "new" company last year, though not really new as such. It's just that the company hasn't been publicly listed before. I have encountered RENK Group AG in other contexts, but the company became more interesting about a year ago when it was listed on the stock market.

Unfortunately, I have not given the stock the time it deserves - and that is something that I intend to change with this article.

Here, I will give you the basic rundown on the company, what it does, and what we can expect from it. It's a specialized engineering/industrial company with segments that I think you will find interesting.

Let's get going.

RENK Group AG - A company in defense with very attractive upside and fundamentals.

Despite its limited size, I believe you will find this company of interest. Defense companies are in high value these days, and there are reasons for it, with Europe "re-arming" itself here.

RENK Group AG is a specialized manufacturer in Germany of transmissions, engines, hybrid drive systems, suspensions, bearings, couplings, and testings systems.

It does this for specialized applications. This means that its products are not used in typical cars but instead in tanks, frigates, icebreakers, and the like. It's also a leading supplier of running gear and specific dampening systems for wheeled military vehicles. It is, in the vast majority, a military supplier and a defense business and has a secondary focus on energy.

Renk employs 3,400 people, has a revenue of just over €1.1B, and is headquartered in beautiful Augsburg, Bavaria, in Germany. The company goes back all the way to the 19th century and Johann Renk, who set up a workshop for gear production in Augsburg in 1873. Like many companies that grew significantly into the world wars, Renk was a major contributor and producer for the German Wehrmacht during WW2, and the company also used forced labour. Following the second world war, the most important customers instead became steel milling companies.

Renk expanded its business following 1950, and had sales of over 50M Deutschmark, and used inorganic growth and M&A's to grow, including German and French companies. The company was renamed the Renk Aktiengesellschaft in 1987 and merged with another business in 1995. After this, the company had some ownership structures that also included MAN. In 2024, the company completed and IPO of €500M and was relisted following a delisting in 2021, back on the Frankfurt exchange in 2024. Triton continues to be a majority shareholder of Renk Group AG to this day.

Based on revenues, you will see that over 70% of the company's revenues are military in origin.

As you can see above, the company has a low leverage of 1.7x, a very good dividend growth, which puts the current yield at around 1% (has to do with valuation, we'll get into that later), and currently enjoys the same very positive order and sales trends we've also seen in other defense companies. In short, the order book is filling quickly.

The main moats and advantages that RENK has is the company's expertise in highly engineered and specialized products. None of what the company does is easily replicable, and then we're not even talking about the difficulty of getting government contracts in these areas but simply the degree of engineering required in tank components. We're talking transmissions for tracked military vehicles, gearboxes for naval military vessels, turbo-gear solutions for industrial sectors, and the like. The company has global market positions in these fields.

In terms of sales, RENK Group is quite well-diversified geographically. But that's not necessarily where the appeal lies. Instead, I invite you to look in what vehicles and vessels the company's products are found.

You will quickly get the, in my view, accurate view that RENK components are found in military applications across the entire world. Its suspensions, bearings, transmissions, and test systems are found across the world. The company also has global market leadership in several key subsegments, this includes being #1 in the world in Naval gearboxes and tank vehicle transmissions. (Source)

That is nothing to sneeze at.

The company's total order backlog has ballooned to €5B as of the end of the fiscal year 2024. The company is using the capital to drive strategic upside, including addressing high-growth markets, driving market, investing in R&D (3% CapEx of revenue over time), and a business model with very high switching costs. Over 180,000 RENK units and components are currently installed worldwide across a diversified customer base.

I would say it's not unfair to expect, based on the current trends, a doubling of company revenue in the medium term, with further growth possible through inorganic M&A. The current tariff and production situation might dilute or make this somewhat less clear, but the company already has a US-based manufacturing footprint, exempting it from some of its tariffs. Take a look at the current base, and you'll see how spread-out it already is.

The growth in worldwide and, above all, US and EU defense budgets will generate significant growth. In 2027, the total defense budgets will amount to over €400B here alone, with the USA and Canada together at almost twice that.

And the company's current portfolio is perfectly positioned for both the military tracked and wheeled vehicles that seem likely to be needed here.

The company's full-year results were excellent. Order intake is up by double digits, revenues are up over 20%, EBIT is up 26% on an adjusted basis, and margins of over 16.5% with RoCE at above 19.5%. The company, like most defense players, is currently "humming". While the company's proposed dividend is really nothing to "phone home" about, it's still good enough for a company in its current situation.

The one thing I have to point out, and it probably won't surprise you given my overall focus on valuation, is the company's pricing in terms of expectations.

But unlike other companies that are currently traded at ballooning and, in some cases, insane multiples, I actually believe we have a better shot at a "good valuation" here.

Furthermore, I believe this company's revenues, upside and earnings to be more likely to increase than others, because this company seems to me to still be at the early stages of its demand cycle, as well as having the potential for significant internal efficiencies, related to working capital as well as other things. (Source)

For that reason, let's look at valuation.

Valuation for RENK Group AG - There might be an upside, but there's also premiumization to account for.

The company achieved its guidance for 2024, and the 2025 guidance supports all of the mid-term targets, including the EBIT growth and the 15% revenue growth. The company expects EBIT to rise over €230M for the year and to continue on its m&A journey, including the post-merger integration work with CGS, Cincinnati Gearing, after closing in April.

The current valuation for the company is 39x P/E. This may sound a lot, but if we estimate it on the basis of expected 2026-2027E results, it's actually closer to 25-26x P/E, which would make this an almost acceptably valued defense player for what it offers.

The problem is that I did not buy the company in December of last year. And while volatile, I'm not sure that I see the company going as low as it was 4 months ago in the near term - not under the current geopolitical circumstances, at least.

This means that while possible to wait for the company to drop, based on growth in earnings of over 20% per year, it's not actually necessarily true that we'll see the company go lower than this - at least not much lower. What would be the catalyst, aside from market realization of the premium? Even the latest few days of tariffs did not make the company go down more than a few percentage points - and I understand why. The company is well-diversified and already has the breadth of production and sourcing to avoid most of the tariffs implemented. It's a very well-prepared player.

So how do we go about valuing this one? At 25x P/E, we have around 6% per year going forward. That's obviously not enough for me. The company is too young to have forecast accuracy and indications of what could happen, but I believe that with the current geopolitics, we may see further growth.

In short, my estimate is that the 20-35% EPS growth per year is a solid base case.

A peer that could be considered relevant here is Rheinmetall, which trades at a 54x P/E but also has better fundamentals. Leonardo is bigger, but also comparable, and trades at roughly 25x, but with a far lower typical "base" valuation on a 20-year basis, also misses estimates quite often, and less than half the EPS growth rate to RENK. The current sentiment on the market seems to be that the company deserves a 30-35x P/E premium. PT's range, based on 11 S&P Global analysts, from €34/share low to €56/share high, to an average of €46/share. Out of 11 analysts, 9 have either a "Buy" or "Outperform rating", signalling high conviction here.

In the end, though, I have to stick to my methods and convictions. This is a BB-rated company that's trading at over 30x P/E, and even at a 30x forward P/E, the rate of return does not reach 15% per year here but rather ends up at around 12.5%. I'm unwilling in any circumstance to value a defense company with this company's fundamentals any higher.

I realize that there's a chance that I might regret this rating going forward, but in my view, unless the company manages to grow 1.3-1.7x as much as the current forecasts, this would be a result of premiumization, which I am unwilling to base a thesis on.

I give the company a PT of €40/share here, implying 24-26x forward P/E - but that means to fulfill my criteria of 15% annualized, the company would have to move to around or below €40/share for the native R3NK ticker here.

That means the company qualifies as a "HOLD", but no higher than that - no matter if you look from the peer perspective or the perspective of future valations.

The ADR for Renk Group AG is RNKGF, a SHARE ADR, meaning it's a 1:1 from EUR to USD, and that means my target comes to $43/share.

My introductory thesis for the company comes in as follows.

Thesis

  • RENK Group AG is a very solid defense manufacturer, with over 70% of the company's annual revenue going directly to defense end markets. This has driven company earnings, order book, and forecasts up. Also, the valuation is now at above 30x PE, which is very high for a BB-rated company with less than 1 year on the market (in this listing).
  • I view the business as fundamentally attractive, and I might regret my conservative take on the business from here on, but I cannot give the company any higher PT than €40/share due to the premiumization and what I view as overvaluation.
  • This PT means that you should buy the company at or below €40/share in order to get a good chance of a 15% annualized RoR, which means that the current rating is "Hold".

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is qualitative
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of €40/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of April 2025.

data.title: Xencor, Inc.: No Major Near Term Catalysts
data.description:
Two years back, I wrote that Xencor (NASDAQ:XNCR) was a once great R&D behemoth emerging with a new pipeline. It was doubtless an R&D behemoth; unfortunately, it hasn’t been able to emerge as well as I hoped. The stock is down 65% from that time, and is trading at all-time lows.

Background

Xencor’s was the R&D behind such big names as Ultomiris, Xevudy and Monjuvi. These are all outlicensed to partners now - Ultomiris to Alexion, a subsidiary of AstraZeneca (AZN), Xevudy to Vir Biotechnology, and Monjuvi to MorphoSys. However, that was a long time ago. The current self-owned pipeline, which is quite long-tailed, is however in early stages. Lacking major late-stage programs, the company has stalled. That is the story of Xencor in brief.

Pipeline

Last time, we saw that the lead asset was Vudalimab, a PD-1 x CTLA-4 targeting bispecific antibody running phase 2 trials for mCRPC and gynecological tumors. However, recently, the company put a pause to this program in order to prioritize “resources to advance other programs.” The company already had safety data from a study of vudalimab in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) and part 1 of another study in non–small-cell lung cancer. However, the mCRPC trial was marred by poor response, an ORR of just 33% with only three confirmed partial responses; worse, there was a grade 5 AE (death) related to the drug.

The prioritisation of Xencor’s portfolio focuses it more on autoimmune diseases now, although even now there are a number of oncology assets. Last year, the company repurposed its bispecific CD20 T cell engager Plamotamab for Multi-drug resistant rheumatoid arthritis, an autoimmune condition, after Janssen scrapped it from a partnership deal in blood cancers.

Another autoimmune asset, XmAb942, will have healthy volunteer data in H1 2025. Then they will move it into a phase 2 trial targeting ulcerative colitis. Another asset, XmAb819, a bispecific T-cell engager targeting ENPP3, an enzyme expressed on clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), will present early data in H2. As the company describes it, this molecule “ engages the immune system and activates T cells for highly potent and targeted lysis of tumor cells expressing ENPP3.”

Another such bispecific T-cell engager targets CLDN6 expressing cancers like ovarian cancer. A Phase 1 dose-escalation study is ongoing. Dose escalation studies are also ongoing for XmAb808 (B7-H3 x CD28), another bispecific antibody. Finally, a number of molecules will begin clinical trials, including Plamotamab (CD20 x CD3), XmAb657 (CD19 x CD3), and a TL1A x IL23 Program.

Among partnered molecules, Amgen’s xaluritamig—a STEAP1 x CD3 XmAb 2+1 bispecific T-cell engager—is being developed for prostate cancer. In Q4 2024, Amgen launched a Phase 3 trial in patients with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) previously treated with taxane-based chemotherapy, triggering a $30 million milestone payment to Xencor, received in 2025. Additional Phase 1 and 1b studies are ongoing to evaluate xaluritamig as monotherapy or in combination in earlier-stage prostate cancer. Data from this program:

Results from a Phase 1 study evaluating xaluritamig in patients with mCRPC were presented at the European Society for Medical Oncology (ESMO) Congress in September 2024. With a median follow-up time of 27.9 months, the median overall survival (OS) was 17.7 months across all cohorts. A PSA90 rate of 45.1% was also observed in high-dose cohorts, and PSA90 response was associated with survival (p = 0.0044)...

Novartis also advanced a partnered asset in Q4, 2024, initiating a Phase 2 study of an investigational antibody incorporating an XmAb Fc domain. This milestone resulted in a $4 million payment to Xencor, received in 2025.

You get the idea. A list of self-owned molecules in various earliest stages of trial, and a list of partnered molecules in somewhat later stages - but where’s the excitement in partnered molecules?

Financials

XNCR has a market cap of $558mn and a cash balance of $497.8mn. Research and development (R&D) expenses for the fourth quarter ended December 31, 2024 were $51.1 million, while general and administrative (G&A) expenses were $14.9 million. Their CFO was ($55.6mn) and capex was ($1.7mn). That gives them an FCF of -$57.3mn and a cash runway of 8 quarters.

Risks

This is a company in transition. Now, that isn’t necessarily a bad thing - except their transition is taking a long time. XNCR has had commercialized molecules - even if partnered - in their portfolio. But now they are offering investors “healthy volunteer data” that doesn’t really mean a thing. A biopharma company doesn’t become believable until it has proof of concept data. That means, data from actual, human patients, ideally in a randomized trial so we know there was an actual treatment effect. Here, we are not going to see proof of concept until probably early 2027. Until then, this company is highly risky.

Bottomline

XNCR is at some of the lowest points of its lifecycle. However, given the total lack of serious catalysts in the next 12+ months, I am sure it has more room to go - downwards. We have no reason to hurry.

data.title: CPI Inflation Drops Yet Again, But Nobody's Celebrating
data.description:
My take on the previous inflation report was titled "This Inflation Drop Is The Best It Gets." I was wrong. For March, inflation based on the consumer price index [CPI] fell even further. The headline figure came in at 2.4% year-on-year [YoY] compared to 2.8% YoY in February.

This, of course, calls for a closer look at the numbers to assess what just happened. Also, what the latest drop now means for monetary policy is also discussed, along with why equity markets tanked yesterday despite the numbers. Finally, some investing options are discussed.

What's behind the decline in inflation?

First, a look at what drove the inflation drop. With the latest decline, inflation is now at its lowest levels in six months (see chart below), coming in lower than the already somewhat muted consensus estimates of 2.6% YoY.

But here's the icing on the cake. The month-on-month [MoM] figure actually turned marginally deflationary, with a 0.1% MoM reduction in prices. The last time the U.S. economy had this experience was during the pandemic period of May 2020, which is almost five years ago now.

So what's behind this surprising print? The biggest reason for this is a big deflation in energy prices of 3.3% YoY and 2.4% MoM. The catch here, though, is that this is hardly a change in direction in YoY figures. Even last month, the segment saw deflation. While it can be argued that the extent of energy deflation has expanded from just 0.3% YoY in February, that's not entirely convincing.

In seven of the past 12 months, this segment has seen deflation. In fact, during August and September last year, energy price deflation was even bigger at 6.8% YoY and 4.9% YoY respectively. Yet, as the chart below shows, headline CPI inflation wasn't particularly different from the latest print. From the MoM perspective, though, the answer is clearer - energy price deflation is the sharpest in a year.

The question that then remains is what other factor is responsible for the latest drop in YoY inflation. The answer lies in core inflation, which fell to 2.8% YoY, the smallest figure in four years. It was only in March 2021 that the number was lower, at 1.6% YoY (see chart below). It does need to be said, though, that even on an MoM basis, the number came in at just 0.1%, the lowest level in nine months, which isn't quite as dramatic an update.

What inflation means for monetary policy

Typically, a drop in inflation of the kind seen in March can be associated with a higher probability of a Fed rate cut. But that's not the case right now. The latest probability accorded to the target rate remaining unchanged at the next FOMC meeting is 70.4% (see chart below). This is a small increase after the figure dropped to 66.7% around the time of the latest jobs report.

So why has the probability dropped? When I analyzed the employment data, I had noted "there's a possibility that the latest rise in probability of a rate cut might not be sustained." And that has happened. The reason for the expectation was that the prospects for the U.S. economy have moved beyond the latest inflation and labor market prints. They are now about the impact of tariffs.

According to JPMorgan, inflation based on the Fed's favored measure, personal consumption expenditure [PCE], is now expected to come in at 2.7% in 2025. This is a 0.2 percentage point increase in the bank's estimates, as a result of the tariff increases. The number isn't unsettling, though. The Fed itself targets headline PCE inflation of 2.7% YoY, as well, for Q4 2025.

The bigger challenge relates to core inflation. JPMorgan now pencils in a core PCE inflation rate of 3.1% this year, up by 0.3 percentage points from earlier. This suggests that there's a risk of the figure tipping over the Fed's target core inflation rate of 2.8% YoY by Q4 2025. Since core inflation is the stickier part of prices, this fact alone can keep the Fed from easing monetary policy further. After all, Fed Chair Powell did recently say that the reserve would like to keep inflation expectations "well anchored."

Implications for equities

With tariffs being top-of-mind, the recently de-coupling between macroeconomic data prints and the stock markets continued yesterday as well. Completely out of line with the inflation data, key indices saw a disastrous performance.

As the chart below shows, the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX, SPY) and NASDAQ 100-Index (NDX, QQQ) saw massive drops of 4.4% and 4.3% respectively yesterday. The Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI, DIA) was relatively less impacted, but even that saw a 2.6% decline.

As I've been pointing out recently, defensives, of course, look like a good choice at this point. Among defensives, my preferred sector has been healthcare, and that stays. In addition, here I'd also like to highlight an ethically debatable but a potentially attractive investment at the present time in tobacco stocks, which have seen an uptick this year. An upcoming article on British American Tobacco (BTI) could be one of the more appealing stocks in the segment, so interested readers might want to look out for it.

Another segment I am bullish on right now is nuclear energy, which is among the sectors that the Trump administration wants to promote. And that's among multiple other reasons for why the sector is attractive right now.

In conclusion

There's much to like about the latest CPI inflation print, but clearly, nobody's celebrating. And that's because the inflation party isn't expected to last. Not with tariff related uncertainties looming large.

As per the latest forecasts, core PCE inflation might even overshoot the Fed's projections for 2025. That, of course, indicates little room for rate cuts. Even the probabilities for a rate cut have narrowed since the latest labor market report despite the latest inflation numbers. And the stock market is looking rather gloomy too.

But for discerning investors, there are opportunities present in the markets that offer relative safety or have potential for an uptick in the foreseeable future.

data.title: OMFL: Current Environment Becomes A New Opportunity For This Dynamic Multifactor Fund
data.description:
The Invesco Russell 1000 Dynamic Multifactor ETF (BATS:OMFL) is one of the funds that relies on an active stock positioning, as it adjusts its exposures based on economic conditions. Despite a relatively higher forward P/E ratio than the Russell 1000 index, OMFL is currently overweight in defensive sectors like consumer staples and financial services.

While this dynamic allocation appears appealing, the reality is that OMFL and most peers, in general, have not consistently outperformed broader benchmark indexes. However, an increasingly complex landscape, with heightened policy risk from potential tariff escalations, persistently high inflation, and global economic uncertainty, presents an opportunity for OMFL, in my view, as the fund has the flexibility to quickly take a more bullish tilt in case there is a favorable resolution in the ongoing trade disputes at some point or, on the contrary, to maintain a defensive positioning for an extended period, and even adopt a more cautious allocation in case of an economic growth deterioration due to a protracted trade war.

ETF Description & Highlights

OMFL is an ETF that tracks the Russell 1000 Invesco Dynamic Multifactor Index, which selects primarily large- and mid-cap stocks based on multiple factors believed to outperform across different market cycles.

This index employs a rules-based strategy that adjusts factor exposures based on prevailing macroeconomic conditions. The index screens securities from the Russell 1000 using a blend of value, momentum, quality, size, and low volatility metrics. Factor configurations shift monthly according to the stage of the economic cycle (recovery, expansion, slowdown, or contraction) identified by economic and sentiment indicators. This dynamic allocation allows the fund to flexibly align with factors that are most likely to outperform in each phase, while thresholds and weight constraints are applied to refine the final portfolio composition and avoid stock concentration.

As of April 3, 2025, OMFL had $4.2 billion in assets under management, invested in a portfolio of 275 companies, with an average market cap of $374.6 billion. The allocation is tilted toward mega caps, which represent 57.5% of total assets, followed by large caps at 30.8%, mid-caps at 10.9%, and small caps at 0.8%. This current allocation marks a sharp shift from the exposure seen at the time of my previous article covering OMFL last July, when most of the fund’s portfolio was allocated to mid-caps and smaller capitalization companies. Meanwhile, for comparison, the Russell 1000 index, represented by the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), has a relatively lower allocation to mega caps at 43.5%, while large caps and mid-caps represent 31.8% and 20.3% of the index, respectively.

OMFL’s top 10 holdings reflect a diversified exposure across sectors, spanning financial services, consumer staples, technology, and communication services. The fund’s largest position is Berkshire Hathaway (6.13%), followed by strong consumer staples plays like Costco (5.28%) and Walmart (5.00%). The fund also includes major tech names such as Microsoft (4.62%), Apple (4.53%), and NVIDIA (3.01%). Consumer-oriented growth names like Amazon (4.43%) and Meta (3.70%) are present alongside financial services names Visa (3.25%) and Alphabet (2.52%).

The following table includes a list of ETFs focused on sector rotation with dynamic allocation that will serve as the benchmark for OMFL in this analysis. The first one is the iShares U.S. Equity Factor Rotation ETF (DYNF), the largest in this group, managing $15.7 billion in assets and employing an active management approach that utilizes five equity style factors, primarily targeting large- and mid-cap stocks. Another relatively large fund is the Main Sector Rotation ETF (SECT), which seeks to outperform the S&P 500 through macroeconomic analysis while primarily investing in large- and mid-cap stocks, with $1.7 billion in assets under management. The SPDR® SSGA US Sector Rotation ETF (XLSR) maintains a similar market size orientation, focusing on favorable sectors within the S&P 500 and managing $588 million. The Burney U.S. Factor Rotation ETF (BRNY) dynamically adjusts its exposure between size and style based on prevailing market conditions, with a greater presence of smaller capitalization companies and $292 million in assets. Completing the peer group, the Anfield U.S. Equity Sector Rotation ETF (AESR) and the RH Tactical Rotation ETF (RHRX) offer targeted sector allocation based on market conditions through investing in sector-specific ETFs rather than individual stocks.

Source: Morningstar, consolidated by the author

From a sector allocation perspective, OMFL’s largest allocation is to technology, with 21.6% of total equities, followed by financial services (16.7%), consumer staples (16.6%), communication services (11.6%), consumer discretionary (10.4%), industrials (9.5%), healthcare (9.1%), utilities (1.8%), basic materials (1.3%), energy (1.1%), and real estate (0.4%). The fund has experienced a significant allocation shift since my previous article on this fund in July 2024, with a strong increase in exposure to technology (+13.4%), consumer staples (+9.8%), and communication services (+8.3%), while the fund has reduced allocation primarily to consumer discretionary (-8.6%), energy (-5.3%) and industrials (-4.4%).

Meanwhile, relative to the Russell 1000 index, OMFL is currently overweight in consumer staples (+10.8%), communication services (+2.4%), and financial services (+2.3%), but underweight in technology (−8.4%), energy (−2.6%), and real estate (−2.2%).

Technology leads OMFL’s sector allocations, making up just over 20% of the fund. This large position is driven by concentrated exposure to software, semiconductors, and hardware. The allocation to software includes top-tier players such as Microsoft (4.6%), Salesforce, and Intuit, reflecting the fund’s tilt toward scalable digital platforms and enterprise solutions. In semiconductors, heavyweights like NVIDIA (3.0%) and Broadcom contribute to strategic positioning in hardware enablement and AI infrastructure, while Apple represents nearly the entire exposure to the hardware sub-industry.

Financial services represent OMFL’s second-largest sector, comprising almost 17% of the portfolio. Unlike traditional broad financial sector exposures, OMFL's tilt leans on specific strategic components. Berkshire Hathaway (6.1%) is the sector’s top holding, while consumer finance is a core pillar industry in this sector, led by Visa and Mastercard, which together account for over 5.6%. A further portion is directed toward the property & casualty insurance segment (1.4%), with names like Progressive and Arch Capital adding exposure to insurance underwriters.

Consumer Staples take the third spot at nearly 17.0% as well, significantly overweight versus the benchmark. The lion’s share is dedicated to hypermarkets and supercenters, with Costco and Walmart together accounting for over 10% of the fund’s weight. Household products and tobacco complement the fund’s allocation to this sector, with Procter & Gamble and Philip Morris highlighting the fund’s current stance in consumer non-cyclicals, known for operational stability and consistent cash flows.

Outside the top three sectors, OMFL has selective exposure to communication and healthcare industries. Exposure to interactive media & services (8.6%) is led by heavyweights such as Meta Platforms and Alphabet, reinforcing a digital-forward stance. In healthcare, pharmaceutical names like AbbVie and Eli Lilly are complemented by leaders in equipment (Intuitive Surgical) and managed care (UnitedHealth), offering a balanced healthcare sector play rooted in both innovation and defensiveness.

Compared to its peer group, OMFL shows a distinct portfolio tilt characterized by selective sector exposures. The most significant overweight is in consumer staples, where OMFL allocates 16.6% versus a 4.5% average among peers, driven by limited exposures in XLSR (0.7%) and SECT (1.7%), which suggests a defensive positioning from OMFL’s side for the time being.

On the other hand, OMFL underweights technology and communication services. Its 21.6% allocation to technology is nearly 10% below peers, while communication services is underweight by 8.9%, with OMFL holding 11.6% compared to 20.4% for the group, as peers like XLSR maintain a heavy allocation to these two sectors

Among the largest sectors, financial services stands out as the most aligned area. OMFL's 16.7% exposure is relatively near the peer group’s 14.4%, indicating a shared view of the sector’s importance in current market conditions.

Valuation Above The Benchmark

With the current market turmoil, stock valuations may be seriously impacted by fast stock price moves and earnings revisions. That said, while market analysts have already started to review earnings forecasts, the coming earnings season that is about to start is likely to bring a series of revised guidance from companies, due to the high degree of uncertainty at this moment. Thus, this valuation analysis obviously does not fully reflect the impact of this potential strong shift in the business dynamics driven by a new tariff regime, but it serves as a reference for the time being.

That being said, as of February 28, 2025, OMFL's forward P/E of 24,0 exceeds the Russell 1000's 20.6, supported by selective sector exposure and strong stock selection. The fund’s most notable contribution to its valuation premium comes from its overweight in consumer staples, where it holds nearly 10 percentage points more than the benchmark. Within this sector, Costco and Walmart, both high-multiple stocks, drive much of the uplift. Financials and industrials also contribute positively to the valuation gap, with sector P/E ratios 6.7 and 3.4 points higher than the benchmark, respectively. On the other hand, OMFL is underweight in information technology, real estate, and healthcare, partially offsetting its premium due to these sectors' typically elevated valuations. At the stock level, names like Visa, Mastercard, and Intuitive Surgical further support OMFL’s valuation profile.

OMFL demonstrates stronger earnings growth momentum compared to the Russell 1000 index across several key metrics. Over the past three years, the fund has delivered historical earnings growth of 15%, more than double the benchmark’s 7.4%, alongside a superior cash flow growth rate of 12.3% versus 6.7% for the index. At the sector level, technology and consumer discretionary stand out with a robust growth profile. Despite being slightly underweight in tech, OMFL captures growth from leaders like Salesforce, Apple, and ServiceNow. In consumer discretionary, the fund benefits from Amazon and Booking Holdings, both of which have strong 3-year and forward growth rates. Meanwhile, consumer staples, a major overweight in OMFL, contributes positively with names such as Walmart and Altria. Even though sectors like energy and real estate lag behind the benchmark in growth, their low weight in the portfolio reduces their impact. Overall, the fund’s average growth is enhanced by strategic overweights in sectors and stocks exhibiting strong long-term earnings growth profiles.

On the profitability side, despite a modest lag in EBITDA margin versus the benchmark (32.2% vs. 33.2%), OMFL’s higher return on equity (49.2% vs. 42.8%) signals strong capital allocation and disciplined balance sheet management across its portfolio. The fund’s strategic overweight to financial services, contributing +4.6% in EBITDA margin and +20.8% in ROE relative to benchmark, plays a pivotal role. Communication services also deliver above-benchmark profitability across both metrics, largely due to contributions from names like Meta Platforms and Alphabet. Industrials and technology further enhance the profile through steady margins and solid ROE. However, consumer staples, while heavily overweighted, contributes negatively to EBITDA margin due to margin-thin retail exposures such as Costco and Walmart. These are partly offset by strong ROE names like Colgate and Procter & Gamble. Overall, OMFL’s overall profile reflects a well-balanced blend of margin strength and high capital returns across the fund’s key sectors.

OMFL’s peer group trades at valuation multiples slightly lower than the Russell 1000 index, despite an average overweight allocation to technology, which may indicate a below-average P/E stock picking, particularly BRNY and XLSR, rather than their allocation alone. Meanwhile, SECT’s P/E is the highest among peers, likely due to its overweight stance on technology.

Struggling To Keep Pace With The Broader Equity Market

As of April 4, 2025, OMFL has delivered a 5-year return of 16.05%, closely tracking its benchmark, the Russell 1000 index. Over the past 3 years, OMFL has slightly lagged the Russell 1000 index (2.11% vs. 4.51%), with similar underperformance in the 1-year period (−7.86% vs. −0.60%), largely due to a pullback in diversified cyclical names. Of note, OMFL outperformed the benchmark in 2022, a down market year, returning −13.97% vs. −19.19%. However, the fund underperformed during market rallies, such as in 2023 and 2024. This pattern is consistent with a defensive bias investment, which tends to outperform in volatile or corrective environments but may lag during stock market rallies.

Compared to peers, OMFL posted a solid return of 16.05% over the past 5 years, outperforming its peer group average of 13.88% with the second-highest gains, only behind DYNF. The fund also ranked among the top performers in 2022, with a return of −13.97% versus the group’s −17.26%, signaling its defensive multifactor construction. Still, the fund trailed peers in 2023 and 2024, particularly as momentum and mega-cap growth rebounded strongly, leading to underperformance relative to peers on a 3-year basis.

OMFL's 3-year beta of 0.95 and 5-year beta of 1.02 indicate modest alignment with market volatility. It captures 92% of the upside and 93% of the downside over five years, which is slightly conservative compared to the Russell 1000 index. The fund also shows a relatively favorable maximum drawdown over the 3- and 5-year periods (−17.17% and −22.04%). Against its peer group, OMFL shows a lower beta than the peer average, although SECT and XLSR have been the least sensitive to market price moves. Meanwhile, OMFL has seen less severe drawdowns than peers' average, particularly compared to DYNF and AESR.

The fund’s average daily volume of $17.59 million is the second-highest within the peer group, while its spread of 0.02% ranks as one of the tightest among peers. The expense ratio of 0.29% places OMFL among the most cost-effective options, despite a high turnover of 267%, the highest in the group, reflecting its monthly factor configuration shifts. On the other hand, its 12-month yield of 1.00% is above the peer group average, but below broader market indexes of nearly 1.2%, while the fund’s tracking error remains elevated, 8.95% (5Y) and 8.27% (1Y), due to the impact of its dynamic factor rotation strategy. Lastly, the fund has maintained Sharpe ratios of 0.77 (5Y) and 0.13 (3Y), which are slightly lower than the peer group average, as peers like DYNF, SECT, and AESR have generated top-tier risk-adjusted returns. In summary, OMFL combines high tradability and a low-cost structure, despite a below-average Sharpe ratio.

Final Thoughts

At the latest check, OMFL’s dynamic approach has structured a portfolio with a strong tilt toward defensive sectors like consumer staples and financial services, and underweight positions in higher-volatility areas such as tech and real estate. While the portfolio's forward P/E is higher than the benchmark, it is backed by strong earnings growth and a robust return on equity of 49.2%, outpacing the Russell 1000 index.

While OMFL’s performance places it among the stronger funds in its category, it has struggled to match the broader index, driven by weaker returns in recent bullish years like 2023 and 2024. On the other hand, the fund has shown relative strength in down years like 2022. This is something that may become an advantage now, as renewed market volatility, driven by trade disputes and fears of a global economic slowdown, has taken center stage.

That said, with OMFL’s track record showing a mixed picture, I take a neutral view on this fund, as near-term visibility remains limited, and the fund’s historical outperformance during harsh conditions, as we are experiencing now, may not compensate for the risk taken in such an uncertain moment for the stock market.

data.title: Nvidia Down 27%: When To Buy (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
For a very long time, and largely influenced by my traditional academic training plus years in fundamentals-based equity research, I ignored the benefits of basic technical analysis — a.k.a. "witchcraft" in the world of portfolio management. Today, I think that obeying a couple of simple rules to sidestep losses while holding a security that is expected to climb over time could do wonders for risk-adjusted performance and portfolio growth over the long haul.

I think that Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) is a great case study. The stock is down 25% since January 2025 as I write this paragraph. By the time you read it, it could be down 50% or back to all-time highs, shockingly, although neither case would invalidate the main premise of this article. I believe that investors who subscribe to the AI growth story could benefit greatly from owning this stock while minimizing the risk of catastrophic failure.

A few words on NVDA first

We all know the core of the NVDA investment thesis. The company is poised to benefit from the widespread adoption of artificial intelligence in the next several years, which could or should lead to massive capex being deployed at compute infrastructure (i.e., processing power at the data center and elsewhere) and software that, arguably, offers the Santa Clara-based tech company a moat in AI.

The story has been coming together quite nicely, as the chart below suggests. Nvidia saw the key line items in its P&L and cash flow statement skyrocket in 2023, with more recent quarterly growth rates of between 38% (free cash flow) and 82% (diluted EPS) still looking quite healthy.

For the fiscal year ending January 2028, NVDA is still expected to deliver earnings per share of $6.59, which would represent a healthy CAGR of 30% over around $3.00 per share delivered in fiscal year 2025. All this expected growth comes at a price of 24.2x fiscal 2026 earnings and a PEG of well less than 1.0x, which I consider to be far from outrageous — and quite a steal, in fact, if NVDA retains its title of growth or even GARP (growth at a reasonable price) stock among traders and investors.

True, there are risks associated with Nvidia's exposure to a possibly severely disrupted global supply chain and the threat of competition in the AI infrastructure arena. As you will see below, these risks can be well-mitigated with the use of sensible technical tools.

Increasing NVDA's risk-adjusted performance

I don't hate the argument that the NVDA price has de-risked enough in the face of widespread market panic in February through early April, which supports a buy today. At the same time, I am not a big fan of "high conviction calls" that are based on personal judgement and biases, especially when volatility across the asset class spectrum is as high as it has been in the past 50 or 60 days — the S&P 500's (SPY) and long-term treasuries' (TLT) daily volatility since President Trump's "Liberation Day" has been a mind-boggling 6% and nearly 2%, respectively, meaning that anything can happen on any given day in the markets at this point.

Much better, in my view, is a trading strategy that looks at two very important metrics that should tell NVDA bulls when to invest with more conviction: price levels and volatility. More specifically, on each trigger:

  • Price levels: Staying invested "through thick and thin" sounds like the ultimate virtue-signaling, high-conviction bullish approach, but it exposes the investor to what could be crippling losses over time. Worth noting, NVDA has corrected by as much as 87% from the peak during the dot-com crash (could AI ever become a bursting bubble?) and 63% even after the AI growth story was already well understood. A good idea is to own NVDA most of the time, but to sell all or most of the position before a relatively small loss becomes a catastrophe.
  • Volatility: Wild swings in share price are the telltale sign that something funky might be going on under the surface. For instance, NVDA's trailing 14-day annualized volatility spiked to as high as 95% on February 13, up sharply from a historical average of 44% and 33% at the start of the year, even before the stock definitively entered correction territory (i.e., a drop of 10% or more from a previous peak), one week later. Selling shares when volatility rises helps to nearly ensure that the stock price fall, if or when it happens, is not too damaging to the portfolio's returns.

My proposed strategy is simple: Own NVDA at nearly all times, but sell four-fifths of the position when either (1) the share price drops below the 200-day moving average or (2) the 14-day trailing annualized volatility reaches 70%, which is at the 90th percentile of Nvidia stock's historical volatility. It's that simple.

But wait, why would I want to head to the sidelines at all on such a solid "growth with a reasonable valuation" story? Wouldn't investors risk missing out on the eventual rallies? It is important to understand that, if NVDA is indeed a good investment idea for the long haul, coming off the recent lows, the lion's share of the potential gains will come from riding the multi-year bullish trend, and not from being onboard for those single-day or single-week share price spikes. Staying invested above the 200-day moving average should help to achieve this goal.

At the end of the day, the proof is in the pudding. I ran a backtest that goes back 20 years — I purposely avoided the dot-com bubble burst period because going back to 1999 would make the strategy described above look unreasonably good. I compared a simple buy-and-hold strategy vs. one that follows the rules outlined above, which I am calling the "Managed Risk" approach, or MR for short. The results are as follows:

Clearly, the results above in green font are exceptional. Surprisingly, even the absolute returns would have been better than buying and holding. This is the case because the portfolio would have dipped less during times of distress, thus eventually climbing off higher lows. The NVDA selloffs of 2008, 2010, 2018, and 2022 would have been mere inconveniences, relatively speaking.

When to buy NVDA

If I were to use the Managed Risk approach, I would be holding only one-fifth of my target NVDA allocation today, per the trading rules. The 200-day moving average of $127 is a solid 10%-plus above the share price of $110, and the 14-day trailing annual volatility is still through the roof, at 102% vs. the 70% cutoff point.

Looking at how things may evolve, I would expect to be fully invested in NVDA at the very end of April, but only if the stock climbs at a consistent pace of 1% per day (or something equivalent to it, as long as volatility does not stay unreasonably high). Conversely, if NVDA and the rest of the market U-turn and head back down, I would expect the Managed Risk strategy to lose only about one-fifth as much as NVDA stock itself, which I find perfectly manageable for a risk-tolerant, growth investor.

data.title: Century Aluminum Is A Risky Bet On The Rebounding Of The US Aluminum Industry
data.description:
Business of Century Aluminum

Century Aluminum (NASDAQ:CENX) produces and sells aluminum products in the United States and Iceland. Its maximum annual capacity is about 1 million metric tons of finished products per year, but the actual capacity is about 700,000 tons, as some production has been suspended.

Most of the assets (3 smelters and the head office) are based in the US, Iceland (1 smelter), Jamaica (a 55% stake in the Jamalco JV, an alumina refinery) and the Netherlands (a plant producing carbon anodes, which are required to make aluminum).

The company has a significant level of self-sufficiency in raw materials due to its control over the Jamalco JV in Jamaica (the venture's remaining 45% is owned by the government). In absolute terms, it is 770,000 tons of alumina at peak capacity (1.4 million tons), which, according to our estimate, covers half of Century Aluminum's needs in this raw material.

In 2024, an average of more than 55% of tons of all primary aluminum shipments came to the United States and 45% to Iceland. The company also earned $252 mln (16% of its total revenue) from alumina sales, taking advantage of rising raw material prices.

Investment thesis

We believe Century Aluminum has several key growth drivers:

  • The protectionist policies of the current administration. In 2024, the US consumed about 4.3 million tons of aluminum. Imports made up more than 80% of the total consumption. About 75% of imported aluminum came from Canada. The Trump administration imposed 25% tariffs on aluminum from other countries. (However, the metal did not fall under the recently announced reciprocal tariffs.) It is important to note that the tariffs apply specifically to finished products (primary aluminum, VAP, aluminum products). Alumina, the raw material needed to produce aluminum, is on the list of critical minerals that have so far escaped tariffs. This means that Century Aluminum, with smelters inside the US, benefits from this situation, as it imports the raw material duty-free, processes it in the US and sells it on the domestic market. Interestingly, the US operates only four smelters (according to a CENX presentation). Of them, 3 are owned by Century.

This affects Century Aluminum's financial performance through regional premiums to LME prices. According to management, the MDW premium (Midwest) has already started to rise. At the beginning of March it rose to $909 per ton from $468 at the end of 2024. Over the medium-term, management sees the potential for the premium to rise to $1,100 per ton ($0.5 per pound).

The company's average selling price is made up of the LME aluminum price, regional premium (MDW or EDPP) and added value per product, if any. Management estimated that an increase in MDW premium by $22.04/T jacked up EBITDA by $9 mln. Therefore, an increase of about $450 per ton in that premium should add about $180 mln to EBITDA, which would equal 90% of the 2024 figure.

  • It is mentioned above that Century Aluminum has a peak capacity of about 1 million tons of finished products per year, while the actual capacity is about 700,000 tons. That's because the company previously mothballed some of its capacity, faced with pressure from foreign suppliers and their price dumping. Consequently, the tariffs present an opportunity for production to rebound. During President Trump's first term, Century Aluminum ramped up capacity utilization (we see it as the ratio of actual shipments to maximum capacity) from ~72%-73% in 2016 to 78%-80% in 2020-2021. We believe the company will again ramp up capacity utilization during Trump's second term, from the current ~66% to 78%-80% toward 2028.
  • The company is going to open another US smelter. It has been reported that Century will receive $500 million in DOE financing for this purpose. However, construction will take several years, and finished products will start coming in 4-6 years, so this is a long-term driver.

Risks

  1. The main risk is the price of aluminum. It recently slumped from $2,700 to $2,300 per ton due to escalating trade wars and recession fears. Management estimates that every $100/ton decrease in the LME price reduces EBITDA by $46 million. This means the recent aluminum price collapse of $400/ton will shave off almost $184 million of EBITDA, offsetting the positive effect of the MDW premium increase.
  2. An additional risk is the likelihood of easing or removal of the trade tariffs. However, we believe that aluminum tariffs will remain at current levels at least until the end of Trump's presidency. We assume this based on experience from his previous term.

Valuation and conclusions

Century Aluminum is a lucrative bet on the rebounding of the US aluminum industry. The company is one of the few certain beneficiaries of Trump's trade policy, aside from the adverse effect of lower aluminum prices. However, if the US doesn't descend into a recession (which is not currently the base-case scenario, according to a consensus forecast), aluminum will quickly rebound in price.

With an average LME aluminum price of $2,700 and an average MDW premium of $956 in 2025, we forecast that EBITDA will reach $304 mln in 2025. (We based this on a conservative outlook for raw material, energy and labor costs.) In our model, we then consider a target EV/EBITDA multiple of 8.5x to be fair. This gives us a target share price of $21 with a BUY rating.

But given the additional risks that have emerged in the form of the recent aluminum price rout, our recommendation is HOLD.
